Matrix multiplication

AUTHOR

Jessica Taberner

SUMMARY
Matrices are rectangular arrays of mathematical objects with entries arranged in rows and columns. Matrices are a very useful concept within mathematics and statistics. You’ll see them used for solving simultaneous equations, modelling data in several variables, and much more. This guide will explain when and how you can multiply together two matrices.

Before reading this guide, it is strongly recommended that you read Guide: Introduction to matrices.

What is matrix multiplication?

A matrix is a rectangular array or table, with entries in rows and columns. Understanding matrices can make solving systems of linear equations more efficient and opens the door to doing mathematics in many dimensions.

Note
Definition of a matrix

A m times n matrix is a rectangular array of m n entries set out in m rows and n columns. You can write it like so:

the 4 by 4 matrix Row 1: Column 1, a sub 11 Column 2, a sub 12 Column 3, dot dot dot Column 4, a sub 1 n Row 2: Column 1, a sub 21 Column 2, a sub 22 Column 3, dot dot dot Column 4, a sub 2 n Row 3: Column 1, vertical ellipsis Column 2, vertical ellipsis Column 3, down right diagonal ellipsis Column 4, vertical ellipsis Row 4: Column 1, a sub m 1 Column 2, a sub m 2 Column 3, dot dot dot Column 4, a sub m n

This matrix has dimension m times n.

The entries in a matrix are usually numbers, but they can be other mathematical objects. Any type of number may be an entry in your matrix, positive or negative, rational or irrational, real or complex. Note here that while entries can be other mathematical objects, for this study guide you will use entries within the real numbers.

Matrices are a fundamental tool within linear algebra, and they have a wide range of real-life applications. They are used in computer graphics, data analysis, search engine optimization, cryptography, economics, robotics, genetics, quantum mechanics, and many more places. Matrices are used anywhere where information in multiple variables needs to be analyzed and calculated efficiently.

A key skill in matrix arithmetic is matrix multiplication. This is different from the scalar multiplication that you saw in Guide: Introduction to matrices, in that instead of multiplying a matrix by a constant, you are multiplying a matrix by another matrix. In this guide, you will see how and when you can multiply together two matrices, as well as how matrix multiplication interacts with other arithmetic operations as seen in Guide: Introduction to matrices.

Matrix multiplication

You can look at how and when you can two multiply matrices together.

Initial example: 2 times 2 matrices

First, let’s see how you can multiply two 2 times 2 matrices via an example. Let A and B be 2 times 2 matrices with entries given below:

A equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 1 negative 1 Row 2: 2 1 B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 3 0 Row 2: 1 negative 1

How can you find their product A B? Since a matrix is defined by its entries, you could think about working out each entry. The idea for the open paren i comma j close parenth entry is to find a ‘product’ of the ith row of A by the jth column of B.

For the open paren 1 comma 1 close parenth entry of A B, you can match the 1st row of A to the 1st column of B, multiply the matched entries together, and add up all the entries. The first row of A is 1 lines Line 1: 1 negative 1, and the first column of B is 1 lines Line 1: 3 1. Here, 1 (from A) and 3 (from B) are matched, and negative 1 (from A) and 1 (from B) are matched. Multiplying these together and adding gives open bracket A B close bracket sub 1 comma 1 equals 1 times 3 plus negative 1 times 1 equals 3 minus 1 equals 2

This process is illustrated in the following equation, with the appropriate row and column highlighted in bold:

A B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: bold 1 negative bold 1 Row 2: 2 1 times the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: bold 3 0 Row 2: bold 1 negative 1 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 1 times 3 plus negative 1 times 1 Column 2, blank Row 2: Column 1, blank Column 2, blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 2 blank Row 2: blank blank

You can now repeat this process to find the other entries. For the open paren 1 comma 2 close parenth entry of A B, you can match the 1st row of A to the 2nd column of B, multiply the matched entries together, and add up all the entries. Doing this in the matrix with the appropriate values highlighted in bold gives

A B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: bold 1 negative bold 1 Row 2: 2 1 times the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 3 bold 0 Row 2: 1 negative bold 1 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 2 Column 2, 1 times 0 plus negative 1 times negative 1 Row 2: Column 1, blank Column 2, blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 2 1 Row 2: blank blank

For the open paren 2 comma 1 close parenth entry of A B, you can match the 2nd row of A to the 1st column of B. Doing this in the matrix with the appropriate values highlighted in bold gives

A B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 1 negative 1 Row 2: bold 2 bold 1 times the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: bold 3 0 Row 2: bold 1 negative 1 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 2 Column 2, 1 Row 2: Column 1, 2 times 3 plus 1 times 1 Column 2, blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 2 1 Row 2: 7 blank

Finally, for the open paren 2 comma 2 close parenth entry of A B, you can match the 2nd row of A to the 2nd column of B. Doing this in the matrix with the appropriate values highlighted in bold gives

A B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 1 negative 1 Row 2: bold 2 bold 1 times the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 3 bold 0 Row 2: 1 negative bold 1 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 2 Column 2, 1 Row 2: Column 1, 7 Column 2, 2 times 0 plus 1 times negative 1 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 2 1 Row 2: 7 negative 1

and this is your final answer!

This process can be generalised to any two 2 times 2 matrices.

Note
Multiplying 2 times 2 matrices

Suppose that A equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: a b Row 2: c d and B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: e f Row 2: g h are two 2 times 2 matrices. Then their product A B is the matrix A B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: a b Row 2: c d times the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: e f Row 2: g h equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, a e plus b g Column 2, a f plus b h Row 2: Column 1, c e plus d g Column 2, c f plus d h

Important

Going ahead, it’s more important to learn the process of how this formula is found, rather than learn the formula itself. This is because this process can be extended to work out general matrix multiplication, and not every matrix is a 2 times 2 matrix!

Tip

The ‘product’ of the ith row of A and the jth column of B to form the open paren i comma j close parenth entry of A B can be recognised as the scalar product of the vectors given by ith row of A and the jth column of B. For more on the scalar product, please see Guide: The scalar product.

Note
Example 1

Let A and B be the following two 2 times 2 matrices. A equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 1 2 Row 2: 3 4 and B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 0 5 Row 2: 6 7

You can follow the process illustrated above to write

A B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 1 2 Row 2: 3 4 times the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 0 5 Row 2: 6 7 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 1 times 0 plus 2 times 6 Column 2, 1 times 5 plus 2 times 7 Row 2: Column 1, 3 times 0 plus 4 times 6 Column 2, 3 times 5 plus 4 times 7

By carrying out these calculations you will have, A B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 plus 12 Column 2, 5 plus 14 Row 2: Column 1, 0 plus 24 Column 2, 15 plus 28 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 12 19 Row 2: 24 43

Unlike for numbers, the order of multiplication matters in matrix multiplication. This is because you take the rows from the left matrix in the product, and columns from the right matrix; these could be different if the order swaps between the left and right matrices! You can see this in the following example.

Note
Example 2

Using the A and B from Example 1, you can compute B A:

B A equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 0 5 Row 2: 6 7 times the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 1 2 Row 2: 3 4 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 times 1 plus 5 times 3 Column 2, 0 times 2 plus 5 times 4 Row 2: Column 1, 6 times 1 plus 7 times 3 Column 2, 6 times 2 plus 7 times 4 By carrying out these calculations you will have, B A equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 0 plus 15 Column 2, 0 plus 20 Row 2: Column 1, 6 plus 21 Column 2, 12 plus 28 equals B A equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 15 20 Row 2: 27 40

You can compare A B and B A.

A B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 12 19 Row 2: 24 43 comma B A equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 15 20 Row 2: 27 40 and here, none of the entries match! Since A B is not equal to B A, this example confirms that matrix multiplication is not commutative in general.

Generalizing this process

Not all matrices are 2 times 2 matrices. How would you multiply two 3 times 3 matrices? Or two 40 times 40 matrices?

The idea in multiplying 2 times 2 matrices was the following:

The idea for the open paren i comma j close parenth entry is to find a ‘product’ of the ith row of A by the jth column of B. This is done by matching entries in the ith row of A to entries in the jth column of B, multiplying the matched entries, and adding them all together.

This applies to matrices A and B of any dimensions, provided that there is a match for all the entries in the ith row of A to the jth column of B. What this means is that two matrices A and B can be multiplied together if and only if the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B. This is because the number of entries in the ith row of A is the same as the number of columns of A, and the number of entries in the jth column of B is the same as the number of rows of B.

Here is a definition of matrix multiplication for more general matrices.

Note
Definition of matrix multiplication

Let A be an m times n matrix and B be an n times p matrix:

A equals the 4 by 4 matrix Row 1: Column 1, a sub 11 Column 2, a sub 12 Column 3, dot dot dot Column 4, a sub 1 n Row 2: Column 1, a sub 21 Column 2, a sub 22 Column 3, dot dot dot Column 4, a sub 2 n Row 3: Column 1, vertical ellipsis Column 2, vertical ellipsis Column 3, down right diagonal ellipsis Column 4, vertical ellipsis Row 4: Column 1, a sub m 1 Column 2, a sub m 2 Column 3, dot dot dot Column 4, a sub m n B equals the 4 by 4 matrix Row 1: Column 1, b sub 11 Column 2, b sub 12 Column 3, dot dot dot Column 4, b sub 1 p Row 2: Column 1, b sub 21 Column 2, b sub 22 Column 3, dot dot dot Column 4, b sub 2 p Row 3: Column 1, vertical ellipsis Column 2, vertical ellipsis Column 3, down right diagonal ellipsis Column 4, vertical ellipsis Row 4: Column 1, b sub n 1 Column 2, b sub n 2 Column 3, dot dot dot Column 4, b sub n p

The product C equals A B is an m times p matrix, where each entry c sub i j of C is given by the following summation formula:

2 lines Line 1: c sub i j equals a sub i 1 b sub 1 j plus a sub i 2 b sub 2 j plus dot dot dot plus a sub i n b sub n j Line 2: blank equals the sum from k equals 1 to n of a sub i k b sub k j In the context of vectors, this is the scalar product of the ith row of A with the jth column of B. You can read more about this at Guide: The scalar product.

For more about the sigma notation in this definition, you can read more about it in Guide: Introduction to sigma notation. It’s important to remember:

Warning

You can only multiply matrices A and B if A has the same number of columns as B has rows, otherwise this operation is undefined.

Warning

As seen in Example 2, matrix multiplication is non-commutative. This means that it is not always true for matrices that A B equals B A. In fact, in some cases, A B may be defined when B A is not defined.

To take a better look at this unusual property, you can now see some examples of this in action. You can use the interactive figure below to investigate multiplying a 2 times 3 matrix by a 3 times 2 matrix (and the other way around). You are able to change the numbers inside the matrices A comma B comma C comma D by clicking on the entry you want to change.

Note
Example 3

Let A be a 2 times 3 matrix and B be a 3 times 2 matrix.

A equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: negative 2 1 3 Row 2: 4 negative 5 2 and B equals the 3 by 2 matrix Row 1: 3 negative 1 Row 2: negative 2 5 Row 3: 1 negative 2

You can work out the product A B. Since A is 2 times 3 and B is 3 times 2, the product A B is a 2 times 2 matrix. You can work out each entry of A B by following the row and column matching as described above.

2 lines Line 1: A B equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: negative bold 2 bold 1 bold 3 Row 2: 4 negative 5 2 times the 3 by 2 matrix Row 1: bold 3 negative 1 Row 2: negative bold 2 5 Row 3: bold 1 negative 2 Line 2: blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, negative 2 times 3 plus 1 times negative 2 plus 3 times 1 Column 2, blank Row 2: Column 1, blank Column 2, blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 5 blank Row 2: blank blank

For the open paren 1 comma 2 close parenth entry of A B, use the first row of A and the second column of B.

2 lines Line 1: A B equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: negative bold 2 bold 1 bold 3 Row 2: 4 negative 5 2 times the 3 by 2 matrix Row 1: 3 negative bold 1 Row 2: negative 2 bold 5 Row 3: 1 negative bold 2 Line 2: blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, negative 5 Column 2, negative 2 times negative 1 plus 1 times 5 plus 3 times negative 2 Row 2: Column 1, blank Column 2, blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 5 1 Row 2: blank blank

For the open paren 2 comma 1 close parenth entry of A B, you can match the 2nd row of A to the 1st column of B.

2 lines Line 1: A B equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: negative 2 1 3 Row 2: bold 4 negative bold 5 bold 2 times the 3 by 2 matrix Row 1: bold 3 negative 1 Row 2: negative bold 2 5 Row 3: bold 1 negative 2 Line 2: blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, negative 5 Column 2, 1 Row 2: Column 1, 4 times 3 plus negative 5 times negative 2 plus 2 times 1 Column 2, blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 5 1 Row 2: 24 blank

Finally, for the open paren 2 comma 2 close parenth entry of A B, you can match the 2nd row of A to the 2nd column of B.

2 lines Line 1: A B equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: negative 2 1 3 Row 2: bold 4 negative bold 5 bold 2 times the 3 by 2 matrix Row 1: 3 negative bold 1 Row 2: negative 2 bold 5 Row 3: 1 negative bold 2 Line 2: blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, negative 5 Column 2, 1 Row 2: Column 1, 24 Column 2, 4 times negative 1 plus negative 5 times 5 plus 2 times negative 2 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 5 1 Row 2: 24 negative 33

and this is your final answer! You don’t have to write all of this out, you could instead write

A B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, negative 2 times 3 plus 1 times negative 2 plus 3 times 1 Column 2, negative 2 times negative 1 plus 1 times 5 plus 3 times negative 2 Row 2: Column 1, 4 times 3 plus negative 5 times negative 2 plus 2 times 1 Column 2, 4 times negative 1 plus negative 5 times 5 plus 2 times negative 2 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 5 1 Row 2: 24 negative 33

Now you can work out B A. Since B is 3 times 2 and A is 2 times 3, the product B A is a 3 times 3 matrix:

B A equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: Column 1, 3 times negative 2 plus negative 1 times 4 Column 2, 3 times 1 plus negative 1 times negative 5 Column 3, 3 times 3 plus negative 1 times 2 Row 2: Column 1, negative 2 times negative 2 plus 5 times 4 Column 2, negative 2 times 1 plus 5 times negative 5 Column 3, negative 2 times 3 plus 5 times 2 Row 3: Column 1, 1 times negative 2 plus negative 2 times 4 Column 2, 1 times 1 plus negative 2 times negative 5 Column 3, 1 times 3 plus negative 2 times 2 By carrying out these multiplications and adding you will have, B A equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: Column 1, negative 6 minus 4 Column 2, 3 plus 5 Column 3, 9 minus 2 Row 2: Column 1, 4 plus 20 Column 2, negative 2 minus 25 Column 3, negative 6 plus 10 Row 3: Column 1, negative 2 minus 8 Column 2, 1 plus 10 Column 3, 3 minus 4 equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: negative 10 8 7 Row 2: 24 negative 27 4 Row 3: negative 10 11 negative 1

Here you can see that not only are the entries in A B not equal to those in B A, but the matrix A B is of different dimension to B A. They are definitely not equal!

Here’s an example where one product of matrices A B is defined, but where the product B A isn’t defined:

Note
Example 4

Now, you can try multiplying a 2 times 3 matrix and a 3 times 1 matrix.

A equals the 2 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 2 negative 1 Row 2: 4 0 3 sans serif and B equals the 3 by 1 column matrix 1 negative 2 0

You can compute A B since, in order to multiply matrices, you require the first matrix to have the same number of columns as our second matrix has rows. Here A has 3 columns, and B has 3 rows. Let’s calculate A B,

A B equals the 2 by 1 column matrix Row 1: 0 times 1 plus 2 times negative 2 plus negative 1 times 0 Row 2: 4 times 1 plus 0 times negative 2 plus 3 times 0 Simplifying gives

A B equals the 2 by 1 column matrix Row 1: 0 plus negative 4 plus 0 Row 2: 4 plus 0 plus 0 equals the 2 by 1 column matrix negative 4 4

So here A B is the 2 times 1 matrix given above.

However, in this example, B A is undefined. This is because you require the first matrix to have the same number of columns as the second matrix has rows, B here only has 1 column, whereas A has 2 rows.

Properties of matrix arithmetic

Now you’ve seen matrix addition and subtraction, scalar multiplication, and matrix multiplication, you can develop some key properties that hold through arithmetic with matrices. These properties rely on arithmetic properties of the set of numbers that give the entries.

Note
Properties of matrix arithmetic

For any three matrices A comma B comma C and any two scalars alpha comma beta, the following properties hold where the operations make sense:

Matrix addition is commutative; that is A plus B equals B plus A.

Matrix addition is associative; that is open paren A plus B close paren plus C equals A plus open paren B plus C close paren.

Scalar multiplication is distributive across matrix addition; that is, alpha times open paren A plus B close paren equals alpha A plus alpha B.

Scalar multiplication is distributive across scalar addition; that is, open paren alpha plus beta close paren times A equals alpha A plus beta A.

Matrix multiplication is associative, that is A of B times C equals A of B C.

Matrix multiplication is distributive across matrix addition; that is A times open paren B plus C close paren equals A B plus A C and open paren A plus B close paren times C equals A C plus B C.

Scalar multiplication can occur at any stage of matrix multiplication; that is alpha of A of B equals alpha of A times B equals A of alpha of B.

You can see proofs of these properties in [Proof sheet: Properties of matrix arithmetic]. Here’s an example of these properties in action:

Note
Example 5

Let’s calculate open paren A plus 2 B close paren times C, for A equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 2 3 Row 2: 0 1 B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 1 2 Row 2: negative 1 negative 3 C equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 1 1 Row 2: 0 1

There are two ways of going about doing this. You could expand the brackets using property (g) above to get open paren A plus 2 B close paren times C equals A C plus 2 B C and work out A C plus 2 B C instead. However, working out open paren A plus 2 B close paren times C involves doing one lot of matrix multiplication, and working out A C plus 2 B C involves two lots of matrix multiplication. Typically, to avoid mistakes you want to reduce the amount of computation you have to do; since matrix multiplication is computationally heavy, doing one set is preferable to doing two!

So you can work out open paren A plus 2 B close paren times C. In Example 8 of Guide: Introduction to matrices you worked out that A plus 2 B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 3 6 Row 2: negative 3 negative 9

Now you can do the matrix multiplication to get:

2 lines Line 1: open paren A plus 2 B close paren times C equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 3 6 Row 2: negative 3 negative 9 times the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: 1 1 Row 2: 0 1 Line 2: blank equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: Column 1, negative 3 times 1 plus 6 times 0 Column 2, negative 3 times 1 plus 6 times 1 Row 2: Column 1, negative 3 times 1 plus negative 9 times 0 Column 2, negative 3 times 1 plus negative 9 times 1 equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 3 3 Row 2: negative 3 negative 12 and this is your final answer. The moral of the story is not to expand brackets unless you really have to!

Quick check problems

Decide whether the following statements are true or false.

Both A B and B A are defined for any two matrices A and B. 
TRUE
FALSE

If A is a p times p matrix and B is a q times p matrix, then B A is a p times q matrix. 
TRUE
FALSE

If A comma B comma C are three matrices, is it always true that A times open paren B plus C close paren equals B A plus C A? Answer: 
TRUE
FALSE
 .

When you multiply together the two matrices B equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 2 2 Row 2: negative 2 2 and C equals the 2 by 2 matrix Row 1: negative 4 3 Row 2: 3 negative 4 you get a 2 times 2 matrix where each column has the same entry throughout. Find the two numbers in the two columns.

Answer: The number in the left column is  and the number in the right column is  .

You are given the following equation the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 1 0 2 Row 2: negative 1 0 1 Row 3: negative 2 1 0 times the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: 0 1 2 Row 2: 1 negative 2 1 Row 3: negative 1 2 0 equals the 3 by 3 matrix Row 1: negative 2 a 2 Row 2: b 1 c Row 3: 1 d negative 3 Find a comma b comma c comma d.

Answer: a equals  , b equals  , c equals  , d equals  .

Further reading

For more questions on this topic, please go to Questions: Matrix multiplication.

For more about how special matrices behave with matrix multiplication, please go to Guide: Matrix multiplication with special matrices.
